options: Any single lowercase letter from a to z
questions.explanation: Character class [a-z] matches any single character in the range. [abc] matches only a, b, or c. Option 1 treats the hyphen as literal. Option 2 requires one or more repetition.

options: One or more of the preceding element
Zero or more of the preceding element
questions.explanation: The + quantifier means at least one occurrence. a+ matches 'a', 'aa', 'aaa'. * means zero or more. Option 1 treats + as literal. Option 2 requires escaping \+.

questions.q: What does ^ match in the regex ^hello?
questions.explanation: ^ anchors to the start of the string. ^hello matches 'hello' only if it begins the string. $ anchors to the end. Option 1 matches any prefix. Option 2 requires literal \^.

options: Creates a capturing group to store 'abc'
questions.explanation: Parentheses () define capturing groups that store matched text. (abc) matches 'abc' and saves it as group 1. [abc] matches single characters. (?:abc) is non-capturing. Option 2 requires [abc] or use a|b|c.
\d is a character class shorthand for [0-9]. It matches single digits. \D matches non-digits. Option 1 treats \d as literal d. Option 2 requires [^0-9].

questions.q: What does the regex a{2,4} match?
questions.explanation: Quantifier {m,n} matches m to n occurrences. a{2,4} matches 'aa', 'aaa', 'aaaa'. a{2} matches exactly 2. Option 1 treats ranges as disjoint. Option 2 is unbounded.

(?:...) is non-capturing—useful for grouping without storing the match. (abc) creates a numbered group. Both match 'abc' but only (abc) stores it. Option 1 reverses the behavior. Option 2 is false—they differ in capture.

options: Positive lookahead—matches if followed by the pattern but doesn't consume it
Matches the pattern and captures it
questions.explanation: (?=...) is a lookahead assertion. It asserts the pattern ahead without including it in the match. hello(?=world) matches 'hello' only if followed by 'world'. (?!...) is negative lookahead. Option 1 captures. Option 2 is lookbehind (?<=).
. is a wildcard matching any character except \n in most engines. To match literal dot, escape as \.. Option 1 treats dot as literal. Option 2 is \d. Option 3 is $.

options: Any single character NOT in a-z (excluding lowercase letters)
Character a followed by hyphen and z
questions.explanation: ^ inside character class [^...] negates the range. [^a-z] matches any non-lowercase letter (digits, uppercase, symbols). Option 1 includes lowercase. Option 2 is incorrect syntax.
